Data representation with R I

Principles and the grammar of graphics

O Rodriguez de Rivera Ortega, PhD

University of Exeter

1 Session overview

1.1 Learning outcomes

  • choose an appropriate graph for categorical, quantitative, temporal and spatial data;
  • build plots in layers using the grammar of graphics;
  • use colour, faceting, shape, size and transparency deliberately;
  • spot common visualisation problems: misleading scales, clutter, overplotting and poor labels;
  • produce publication-ready graphics using ggplot2.

2 Setup

2.1 Packages used in this practical

Run this once before the practical if packages are not already installed.

install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse", "palmerpenguins", "gapminder", "datasauRus",
  "patchwork", "scales", "viridis", "maps", "GGally", "plotly"
))

The practical mostly uses package datasets, so it does not depend on internet access once packages are installed.

library(tidyverse)
library(palmerpenguins)
library(scales)

theme_set(theme_minimal(base_size = 12))

2.2 Example datasets

We will use:

  • palmerpenguins::penguins: ecological measurements from three penguin species;
  • gapminder::gapminder: country-level time series for population, GDP per capita and life expectancy;
  • datasauRus::datasaurus_dozen: a reminder that summary statistics alone can be misleading;
  • ggplot2::mpg and ggplot2::economics: built-in examples for additional practice.
penguins_clean <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(species, island, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm,
          flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, sex)

glimpse(penguins_clean)
## Rows: 333
## Columns: 8
## $ species           <fct> Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adelie, Adel…
## $ island            <fct> Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgersen, Torgerse…
## $ bill_length_mm    <dbl> 39.1, 39.5, 40.3, 36.7, 39.3, 38.9, 39.2, 41.1, 38.6…
## $ bill_depth_mm     <dbl> 18.7, 17.4, 18.0, 19.3, 20.6, 17.8, 19.6, 17.6, 21.2…
## $ flipper_length_mm <int> 181, 186, 195, 193, 190, 181, 195, 182, 191, 198, 18…
## $ body_mass_g       <int> 3750, 3800, 3250, 3450, 3650, 3625, 4675, 3200, 3800…
## $ sex               <fct> male, female, female, female, male, female, male, fe…
## $ year              <int> 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007, 2007…

3 Why visualise data?

3.1 Visualisation is not decoration

A good graph helps us to answer a question.

Before plotting, ask:

  1. What is the question?
  2. What type of variables do I have?
  3. What comparison or pattern should the reader see first?
  4. What should be removed because it distracts from that pattern?

3.2 Same summaries, different patterns

The Datasaurus Dozen contains datasets with very similar means, standard deviations and correlations, but very different visual patterns.

library(datasauRus)

datasaurus_dozen %>%
  group_by(dataset) %>%
  summarise(
    mean_x = mean(x),
    mean_y = mean(y),
    sd_x = sd(x),
    sd_y = sd(y),
    correlation = cor(x, y),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  slice(1:6)

3.3 Plot the data

ggplot(datasaurus_dozen, aes(x = x, y = y)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8, size = 1.4) +
  facet_wrap(~ dataset, ncol = 4) +
  labs(
    title = "Always plot the data",
    subtitle = "Similar summary statistics can hide very different structures",
    x = NULL,
    y = NULL
  )

3.4 Common problems

  • Ugly: aesthetic choices make the plot hard to read, but the message is still correct.
  • Bad: perceptual choices make the plot confusing or misleading.
  • Wrong: the plot is mathematically incorrect or the visual encoding contradicts the data.

Examples:

  • bar charts with a truncated y-axis;
  • colour scales that imply order where none exists;
  • 3D pie charts;
  • too many groups in one panel;
  • legends and labels that do not explain units.

4 The grammar of graphics

4.1 Anatomy of a ggplot2 graph

A ggplot2 graph is built from layers:

ggplot(data = DATA, mapping = aes(x = X, y = Y)) +
  GEOM_FUNCTION() +
  SCALE_FUNCTION() +
  COORDINATE_FUNCTION() +
  FACET_FUNCTION() +
  THEME_FUNCTION()

Core components:

  • data: the dataset;
  • aesthetics: mapping variables to visual features;
  • geometries: points, lines, bars, boxes, polygons;
  • scales: colour, size, axis transformations;
  • facets: small multiples;
  • themes: non-data appearance.

4.2 Start with a mapping

ggplot(
  data = penguins_clean,
  mapping = aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = flipper_length_mm)
)

The plot is empty because we have said what variables to use, but not how to draw them.

4.3 Add a geometry

ggplot(penguins_clean, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_point()

4.4 Improve readability

ggplot(penguins_clean, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = flipper_length_mm)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Bill length and flipper length in Palmer penguins",
    x = "Bill length (mm)",
    y = "Flipper length (mm)"
  )

4.5 Map species to colour

ggplot(penguins_clean,
       aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = flipper_length_mm, colour = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.8, size = 2.5) +
  labs(
    title = "Species structure is visible after adding colour",
    x = "Bill length (mm)",
    y = "Flipper length (mm)",
    colour = "Species"
  )

4.6 Add a trend carefully

ggplot(penguins_clean,
       aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = flipper_length_mm, colour = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  labs(
    title = "Linear trends by species",
    x = "Bill length (mm)",
    y = "Flipper length (mm)",
    colour = "Species"
  )

4.7 Facet into small multiples

ggplot(penguins_clean,
       aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = flipper_length_mm, colour = species)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 2) +
  facet_wrap(~ island) +
  labs(
    title = "Small multiples reduce visual clutter",
    x = "Bill length (mm)",
    y = "Flipper length (mm)",
    colour = "Species"
  )

5 Design principles

5.1 Match plot type to variable type

Variable relationship Useful plots
One categorical variable bar chart
One quantitative variable histogram, density, dotplot, boxplot
Categorical vs quantitative boxplot, violin, jittered points
Quantitative vs quantitative scatterplot, smooth, hexbin
Time vs quantitative line plot
Space point map, choropleth, raster/heatmap
Many variables facets, colour/shape/size, PCA, heatmap

5.2 Design checklist

Before showing a graph, check:

  • Does the title state the main message?
  • Are axes labelled with units?
  • Does the legend use clear names?
  • Is colour necessary and accessible?
  • Is there overplotting?
  • Are scales honest? For bars, zero should normally be visible.
  • Is the graph simpler than the table it replaces?

6 Short exercise

6.1 Exercise 1: build a plot in layers

Use penguins_clean and create a plot showing the relationship between body_mass_g and flipper_length_mm.

Tasks:

  1. Start with ggplot() and a mapping.
  2. Add points.
  3. Colour by species.
  4. Add a linear trend.
  5. Add informative labels.
  6. Facet by sex.

6.2 Discussion

After completing the exercise, discuss:

  • What is the main pattern?
  • Does the pattern differ by species or sex?
  • Is colour or faceting doing more useful work?
  • What would you change before using the graph in a report?
